latest_docs.snippet: Explains GGUF quantization with llama.cpp, notes support for 1.5–8-bit quants and that GGUF+llama.cpp is optimized for CPU-only inference, showing ~20 tok/s on a 2-core Haswell Colab CPU at Q4 levels.

Describes GGUF as a binary format designed specifically for quantized LLM storage and efficient CPU inference, stating that GGUF plus llama.cpp has become the de facto standard for consumer hardware deployments.

prompt: Collect detailed, recent CPU-only benchmarks and optimization data for llama.cpp (and forks), CTranslate2, and competing runtimes; look for weak-hardware-focused guidance.
user_goal: Compare the best open-source CPU LLM inference stacks for weak hardware in 2026 and recommend one, using independent benchmarks, recency checks, and contradiction resolution.
known_facts: A 2026 CEUR workshop paper systematically benchmarks seven 1–24B models on three CPU-only systems with llama.cpp Q4_K GGUF, reporting ~120/45/15 tok/s for 1B/7.6B/24B on a modern Xeon and ~25/8/2 tok/s on an older Xeon, with 0.5–13.4 GB memory footprints.

The same study concludes that 4-bit GGUF quantization via llama.cpp is near state-of-the-art for CPU LLM inference and that 1–4B models are ideal for low-thread budgets, 7–8B for mid, and 13B+ for many cores.

ik_llama.cpp is a fork of llama.cpp focused on better CPU matmuls and adds SOTA quants, with 2–4x prompt-processing speedups and significant TG gains on AVX2/ARM NEON, confirmed by December 2024 benchmarks and 2026 user tests on Zen5 CPUs.

Llamafile achieves 30–500% faster prompt processing than upstream llama.cpp on some CPUs (especially AVX-512/BF16), with up to 10x speedups in prefill, albeit with some reported differences in logits vs llama.cpp.

CTranslate2 benchmarks show 2–3x+ speed and major memory reductions vs OpenNMT-tf/py on CPU translation tasks (e.g., 593 vs ~280 tok/s and half the memory), and it supports Llama/Mistral/Gemma/Qwen2 in addition to translation models.
open_gaps: Need independent industry/production comparisons explicitly recommending stacks for CPU inference (e.g., vLLM alternatives).
Need clear data on MLX/MLC-LLM vs llama.cpp on Apple Silicon to understand when they beat llama.cpp vs when they are niche.
Need cross-ecosystem context for OpenVINO or other Intel-optimized CPU stacks relative to llama.cpp on CPUs.
Need to verify how community/front-end tools (Ollama, LM Studio, GPT4All) relate to these runtimes on CPU.

contradictions.snippet: Academic CPU-only results say llama.cpp with 4-bit GGUF is near state of the art on Xeon CPUs, while MLC-LLM GPU benchmarks show MLC exceeding llama.cpp on GPUs, raising questions about CPU vs GPU context.

latest_docs.snippet: CEUR 2026 paper on CPU-only deployment with llama.cpp: detailed tokens/s and memory footprints for 1–24B models on E5-2695 v2, Xeon 8480+, and i7-13700H; shows feasibility of multi-agent CPU deployments under 32 GB RAM using Q4_K GGUF.
ik_llama.cpp README: focuses on better CPU matmuls (AVX2/NEON) and new SOTA quants, typically 2x+ faster prompt processing and faster TG vs mainline llama.cpp, with Bitnet and deep Qwen support.

Reddit Zen5 laptop test: ik_llama.cpp delivers ~5x faster prompt processing and ~1.7x faster TG than llama.cpp on a 4.8B Qwen3.5 IQ4_XS model at 10 threads, highlighting big CPU-only gains on modern laptops.

Older but illustrative CTranslate2 benchmarks: achieves ~593 tok/s vs ~280 tok/s for OpenNMT-TF/OpenNMT-py and with lower memory, showing its CPU runtime is highly optimized for Transformer translation models.

prompt: Cross-check ecosystem-level 2025–2026 surveys (vLLM alternatives, local LLM guides) plus Apple/Intel-specific analyses to resolve contradictions and solidify a recommendation for weak CPU-only hardware.
user_goal: Compare the best open-source CPU LLM inference stacks for weak hardware in 2026 and recommend one, using independent benchmarks, recency checks, and contradiction resolution.
known_facts: A 2026 vLLM-alternatives article positions llama.cpp as best for consumer hardware and CPU inference, noting vLLM’s CPU mode is weak and emphasizing llama.cpp’s years of CPU optimization plus GGUF quantization flexibility, with concrete CPU benchmarks (e.g., Llama 3.1 8B Q4_K_M at ~28 tok/s, 5.2 GB on Ryzen 9 5950X).

The same article lists CTranslate2 as best for CPU efficiency in a table but primarily in the context of translation and server-style workloads, not general consumer local LLM usage, and also highlights OpenVINO as a strong Intel-specific CPU engine.

A 2025 Apple Silicon study finds MLX achieves the highest throughput and MLC-LLM the lowest TTFT on M2 Ultra, while llama.cpp remains highly efficient for single-stream use; a 2026 blog quantifies MLX-LM as 20–40% faster than llama.cpp on M-series Macs.

Local-LLM guides and StackOverflow answers for CPU-only inference recommend llama.cpp / llama-cpp-python plus GGUF quantization as the default way to run quantized models on CPU, citing ease-of-use and early support for new quants.
Quantization guides aimed at CPU deployment explicitly highlight GGUF/ GGML plus Q4_K_M as the best default for CPU inference, with AVX2/AVX-512 acceleration and Q3/Q2 variants for ultra-weak CPUs.
OpenVINO INT8 on Xeon 8480+ can reach ~32 tok/s for Llama 3.1 8B vs ~14 tok/s with llama.cpp CPU and ~8 tok/s with vLLM CPU, but this is Intel-specific tuning and requires a separate toolchain.

contradictions.snippet: Apple-centric studies show MLX/MLC-LLM outperforming llama.cpp on M-series Macs, whereas cross-platform sources describe GGUF+llama.cpp as the de facto consumer standard; the resolution is that MLX is Apple-only while GGUF+llama.cpp is hardware-agnostic.

OpenVINO and Intel INT4 runtimes can sometimes exceed llama.cpp throughput on specific Xeons, yet the CEUR study and vLLM-alternatives guide still treat llama.cpp as near-SOTA and the default for CPU inference on general hardware; the resolution is that OpenVINO is Intel-specific, while llama.cpp targets heterogeneous CPUs including ARM and older x86.

vLLM-alternatives table calls CTranslate2 best for CPU efficiency, but elsewhere in the same article llama.cpp is recommended for consumer hardware and CPU, suggesting CTranslate2’s advantage is primarily for translation/batch workloads rather than general local chat LLMs on weak hardware.

latest_docs.snippet: 2026 vLLM-alternatives article: compares 15 engines, explicitly stating that CPU inference is not vLLM’s strength, that llama.cpp is best for consumer hardware and CPU with unmatched memory efficiency for quantized models, lists CTranslate2 as CPU-efficiency-focused, MLX as Apple Silicon-focused, and OpenVINO as Intel-specific CPU optimization.

2025 Apple Silicon comparative study: evaluates MLX, MLC-LLM, llama.cpp, Ollama, and PyTorch MPS; finds MLX highest throughput, MLC-LLM best TTFT, and llama.cpp highly efficient for lightweight single-stream use, clarifying their relative strengths on M2 Ultra.

2026 Apple blog: shows MLX-LM typically 20–40% higher token throughput than llama.cpp on M-series Macs and notes that the gap widens at long context lengths because MLX keeps KV cache fully in unified memory, but acknowledges that llama.cpp is still competitive and cross-platform.

2025 CPU quantization guide: recommends GGUF/GGML formats for CPU, with Q4_K_M as the default sweet spot, Q5_K_M for quality on stronger CPUs, and Q2/Q3 for ultra-weak hardware, explicitly tying these to AVX2/AVX-512 acceleration.

StackOverflow answer on running quantized GGUF models on CPU advises using llama-cpp-python as the simplest way to load GGUF, configure threads, and run CPU-only inference from Python, reflecting ecosystem maturity.

ClickHouse is widely used in observability stacks (Uptrace, OneUptime) and is praised for handling high-cardinality dimensions well.
GreptimeDB is moving toward 1.0 GA with explicit design work on high-cardinality primary keys and resource optimization.

contradictions.description: Vendor benchmarks (e.g., VictoriaMetrics vs Mimir) may be biased; need third-party confirmation of resource-efficiency claims.
Some sources say Prometheus and its ecosystem 'can’t handle' high cardinality, while others show billion-series scale with Mimir or millions of active series with VictoriaMetrics.
InfluxDB marketing for v3.0 claims improved high-cardinality behavior, whereas historical and third-party reports emphasize serious cardinality problems in v1/v2.

latest_docs.snippet: LinkedIn engineer’s 2026 Mimir vs VictoriaMetrics benchmark on ~5.5M active series, 360k samples/s, showing similar p50, lower p99 for VictoriaMetrics, and Mimir hitting memory limits under 5x load while Victoria saturated CPU but stayed up.

Uptrace’s 2025 'Top 5 Time-Series Databases for High-Cardinality Metrics' article benchmarking Uptrace’s ClickHouse backend versus InfluxDB and Prometheus, highlighting ClickHouse’s strength for high-cardinality workloads.

OneUptime’s 2026 guide on replacing InfluxDB with ClickHouse for metrics, stating InfluxDB struggles with high-cardinality metrics and demonstrating ClickHouse schemas using LowCardinality and Map types.
CrateDB’s 2025 survey of top TSDBs in 2026, discussing InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, ClickHouse, QuestDB, and VictoriaMetrics, with ClickHouse praised for 'unlimited cardinality and long-term retention'.
TimescaleDB vs InfluxDB whitepaper describing benchmark methodology and showing Timescale often outperforms InfluxDB v1.8 as cardinality increases, especially for complex queries.
Tiger Data’s in-depth comparison of TimescaleDB vs InfluxDB, concluding Timescale generally outperforms InfluxDB for simple rollups and complex aggregates at both low and high cardinality.

Same Tiger Data article’s section on handling high-cardinality data, noting ClickHouse handles it well by design, and that InfluxDB 3.0’s new Parquet-based storage improves upon earlier cardinality issues.
ClickHouse’s 2025 overview of top infrastructure monitoring tools, describing VictoriaMetrics as a high-performance, resource-efficient Prometheus remote-storage backend with operational simplicity.
Apica’s 2026 post on high-cardinality metrics at scale, describing how cloud-native and AI agents explode metrics cardinality and asserting that traditional TSDBs weren’t designed for this reality.
